title: Acquired coagulation disorders
body_html:
Acquired coagulation disorders develop after birth secondary to systemic disease, drugs, or autoantibodies. They are more common than inherited disorders and often involve multiple factor deficiencies.

Key acquired coagulation disorders include:
  • Vitamin K deficiency
  • Liver disease associated coagulopathy
  • Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)
  • Autoantibody inhibition of coagulation
  • Other miscellaneous causes e.g. transfusion syndrome

These are considered in more detail below:

Vitamin K deficiency

Causes:
  • Haemorrhagic disease of the newborn
  • Biliary obstruction (fat malabsorption)
  • Malnutrition/malabsorption (e.g. pancreatic, bowel disease)
  • Vitamin K antagonist therapy (warfarin)
  • Prolonged antibiotic use (↓ gut flora)
Pathophysiology:
Reduced activity of vitamin K–dependent factors (II, VII, IX, X, protein C, protein S)
Labs:
  • Prolonged PT (early)
  • Prolonged aPTT in severe cases
  • Normal platelet count
Clinical features:
  • Mucocutaneous bleeding
  • post-surgical haemorrhage
  • neonatal bleeding

Vitamin K is a fat-soluble vitamin, obtained from:
  • Dietary sources (especially green leafy vegetables)
  • Bacterial synthesis in the gut (colon flora)
Deficiency can present in two main contexts:
  • Newborns due to low placental transfer, immature liver, low vitamin K stores, and lack of gut flora (known as haemorrhagic disease of the newborn)
  • Later life due to malnutrition, fat malabsorption (biliary obstruction, pancreatic disease), prolonged antibiotics, or vitamin K antagonist therapy (warfarin)


Liver disease-associated coagulopathy

Pathophysiology:
  • Decreased synthesis of clotting factors (all except factor VIII)
  • Decreased fibrinogen in severe hepatocellular failure
  • Dysfibrinogenemia (abnormal fibrinogen function)
  • Increased Plasminogen activator → enhanced fibrinolysis
  • Decreased Thrombopoietin → thrombocytopenia
  • Hypersplenism (portal hypertension) → splenic sequestration of platelets
  • Biliary obstruction → vitamin K malabsorption
  • DIC may complicate severe disease
  • Net effect: Can be prothrombotic or haemorrhagic
Labs:
  • Prolonged PT
  • Prolonged aPTT
  • Low fibrinogen (in severe cases)
  • Thrombocytopenia


Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)

Pathogenesis:
  • Widespread activation of coagulation → excess thrombin → fibrin deposition and platelet consumption
  • Simultaneous activation of fibrinolysis → fibrin degradation products (FDPs) interfere with fibrin polymerisation
  • Net result: consumptive coagulopathy (bleeding + thrombosis)
Causes:
  • Infection: Gram-negative sepsis, meningococcal sepsis, malaria
  • Malignancy: acute leukaemia, adenocarcinoma
  • Obstetric: amniotic fluid embolism, placental abruption, eclampsia
  • Hypersensitivity: anaphylaxis, incompatible transfusion
  • Tissue damage: trauma, burns, surgery
  • Vascular: leaking prosthetic valves, bypass surgery, aneurysms
  • Misc: liver failure, snake bites, pancreatitis, hypothermia, massive haemorrhage
Clinical features:
  • Bleeding: from venepuncture sites, wounds, GI tract, mucosa, urogenital tract, obstetric bleeding
  • Thrombosis: skin necrosis, renal failure, gangrene, cerebral ischaemia
Labs:
  • Decreased platelets
  • Decreased fibrinogen
  • Increased FDPs / D-dimer
  • Prolonged PT, aPTT, TT
  • Evidence of haemolysis
Management:
Treat underlying cause, supportive transfusion (FFP, platelets, cryoprecipitate); anticoagulation in select thrombotic cases


Autoantibody inhibition of coagulation

Specific inhibitors:
  • Acquired haemophilia A (autoantibodies to factor VIII) → severe bleeding
Non-specific inhibitors:
  • Lupus anticoagulant / antiphospholipid antibodies (often prothrombotic despite prolonged aPTT)


Miscellaneous causes
  • Diseases with M-protein production (e.g. multiple myeloma) → interference with coagulation
  • L-asparaginase therapy (decreased protein synthesis)
  • Anticoagulant therapies (Heparin, defibrinating agents e.g. snake venom, thrombolytic therapy)
  • Massive transfusion syndrome → dilutional coagulopathy (loss of clotting factors + platelets)
  • Uremia → platelet dysfunction (normal PT/aPTT, prolonged bleeding time)


Summary table

CausePathophysiologyLabsClinical Features
Vitamin K deficiency (newborn, biliary obstruction, malabsorption, warfarin)↓ vitamin K–dependent factors (II, VII, IX, X, protein C & S)Prolonged PT (early), prolonged aPTT if severe, normal plateletsMucocutaneous bleeding, surgical/postpartum haemorrhage, neonatal bleeding
Liver disease↓ synthesis of clotting factors (except VIII), ↓ factor V & fibrinogen, dysfibrinogenemia, ↑ fibrinolysis, ↓ thrombopoietin, hypersplenismProlonged PT & aPTT, ↓ fibrinogen, thrombocytopeniaBruising, mucosal bleeding, variceal haemorrhage, paradoxical thrombosis
DICWidespread thrombin activation → fibrin deposition, platelet & factor consumption, ↑ fibrinolysis → FDPs↓ Platelets, ↓ fibrinogen, ↑ D-dimer, prolonged PT, aPTT, TT, evidence of haemolysisBleeding (venepuncture, mucosa, GI, obstetric) + thrombosis (skin necrosis, renal failure, CNS ischaemia)
Acquired hemophilia (specific inhibitor)Autoantibodies against factor VIIIIsolated prolonged aPTT, normal PT, normal plateletsSevere spontaneous bleeding (soft tissue, mucosa, GI, GU)
Lupus anticoagulant (non-specific inhibitor)Antiphospholipid antibodies interfere with phospholipid-dependent assaysProlonged aPTT (not corrected on mixing), PT normal, platelets normalOften prothrombotic (DVT, PE, miscarriage) despite prolonged aPTT
Miscellaneous (M-protein, L-asparaginase, heparin, thrombolytics, massive transfusion, uremia)Protein interference, ↓ synthesis, drug effects, dilution, platelet dysfunctionVariable: heparin → prolonged aPTT, thrombolytics → low fibrinogen, massive transfusion → low platelets & factors, uremia → normal PT/aPTT but prolonged bleeding timeBleeding tendency, sometimes thrombosis (esp. heparin-induced thrombocytopenia)
